title: CodeGen: An Open Large Language Model for Code with Multi-Turn Program Synthesis

structured.summary: The paper presents CODEGEN, an open-source family of large autoregressive language models trained on natural language and diverse code datasets, scaling up to 16.1B parameters. It introduces a multi-turn program synthesis paradigm and a novel Multi-Turn Programming Benchmark (MTPB), demonstrating that multi-turn factorization improves model understanding and code generation success, especially for larger models and complex problems.

strengths.point: Development and public release of large open-source code models and training infrastructure
strengths.evidence: CODEGEN models up to 16.1B parameters and the JAXFORMER training library are released for community use.

strengths.point: Introduction of a multi-turn program synthesis paradigm with a novel benchmark
strengths.evidence: The Multi-Turn Programming Benchmark (MTPB) with 115 expert-written multi-turn problems enables quantitative evaluation of multi-turn synthesis.

strengths.point: Empirical evidence that multi-turn factorization improves model understanding and synthesis success
strengths.evidence: Multi-turn prompts yield lower perplexity and roughly 10% higher pass rates than single-turn prompts, especially for larger models and harder problems.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient comparison to prior multi-turn or interactive program synthesis literature
weaknesses.evidence: The paper lacks citations or discussion of related work on interactive or iterative program synthesis, such as neural dialogue agents or program repair frameworks.

weaknesses.point: Limited evaluation scope focused only on Python and English prompts despite multilingual training data
weaknesses.evidence: All benchmarks (HumanEval, MTPB, MBPP) evaluate Python code with English prompts, leaving generalization to other languages or user intents untested.

weaknesses.point: Lack of ablation studies and detailed training hyperparameters hinders reproducibility and understanding of design choices
weaknesses.evidence: No experiments explore alternative dataset mixing or curriculum orders; critical architectural and training details (e.g., batch size, optimizer settings) are omitted.

weaknesses.point: No human or user studies to validate multi-turn interaction usability and naturalness
weaknesses.evidence: Despite motivating multi-turn synthesis as easing user intent specification, no experiments involve real or simulated users to assess interaction quality.

weaknesses.point: Absence of systematic error analysis, computational resource reporting, and robustness evaluations
weaknesses.evidence: No breakdown of failure modes, inference latency, token efficiency, or performance scaling with longer multi-turn dialogues is provided.

questions.question: How does CODEGEN’s multi-turn program synthesis compare quantitatively and conceptually to existing interactive or iterative synthesis approaches?
questions.motivation: Clarifying novelty and positioning would strengthen the paper's contribution claims and guide future research.

questions.question: Can the multi-turn paradigm and CODEGEN models generalize effectively to other programming languages and non-English user intents?
questions.motivation: Demonstrating broader applicability is important for practical adoption and assessing model versatility.

questions.question: What are the computational trade-offs and latency implications of multi-turn synthesis compared to single-turn generation?
questions.motivation: Understanding efficiency and responsiveness is critical for real-world deployment and user experience.

critique_points.point_001: Insufficient comparison to prior multi-turn or interactive program synthesis literature. The paper lacks citations or discussion of related work on interactive or iterative program synthesis, such as neural dialogue agents or program repair frameworks
critique_points.point_002: Limited evaluation scope focused only on Python and English prompts despite multilingual training data. All benchmarks (HumanEval, MTPB, MBPP) evaluate Python code with English prompts, leaving generalization to other languages or user intents untested
critique_points.point_003: Lack of ablation studies and detailed training hyperparameters hinders reproducibility and understanding of design choices. No experiments explore alternative dataset mixing or curriculum orders; critical architectural and training details (e.g., batch size, optimizer settings) are omitted
critique_points.point_004: No human or user studies to validate multi-turn interaction usability and naturalness. Despite motivating multi-turn synthesis as easing user intent specification, no experiments involve real or simulated users to assess interaction quality
critique_points.point_005: Absence of systematic error analysis, computational resource reporting, and robustness evaluations. No breakdown of failure modes, inference latency, token efficiency, or performance scaling with longer multi-turn dialogues is provided
